text.title: EF Core Patterns Failure Modes
text.summary: Four EF Core antipatterns. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: List query with `Include(o => o.Items).Include(o => o.Discounts)` produces a JOIN over two collections. Page load returns 50k rows for 100 orders × 10 items × 50 discounts.

root-cause.source: EF Core docs — Single vs split queries

antipattern.root-cause: Default single-query Include over multiple collections produces Cartesian product.
antipattern.fix: Append `.AsSplitQuery()`. EF emits separate SELECTs and merges client-side. Document in code why it's split (link to this rule).

antipattern.symptom: List endpoint loads 1000 entities, materializes change-tracker entries, and serialises them. Each request leaks ~20MB before GC. Throughput collapses.
antipattern.fix: Add `.AsNoTracking().Select(o => new OrderDto(o.Id, o.Total))` to the query. Materialize via `ToListAsync(ct)`.

antipattern.symptom: `UseLazyLoadingProxies()` enabled; controller returns entities; serializer touches navigation, EF fires N+1 SQL after the DbContext has been disposed. `ObjectDisposedException` mid-response.
antipattern.root-cause: Lazy loading + entity-in-response forces a hidden runtime contract that breaks when DbContext is request-scoped.
antipattern.fix: Disable `UseLazyLoadingProxies`. Use explicit `Include` or projection. Make navigations require an explicit eager-load path.

antipattern.symptom: Engineer renames a migration file or edits its `Up()` after merge to fix a typo. Deployed environments diverge: prod has applied old version, staging has new.
antipattern.root-cause: Migration treated as code instead of as an immutable schema-history record.
antipattern.fix: Revert the edit. Add a new migration `FixTypoInOrdersTable` that applies the correction. Pin policy in CONTRIBUTING.
